title: Medical error: disclose to the surrogate decision-maker
vignette: An 82-year-old man with a history of advanced Alzheimer dementia is brought to the hospital by his daughter, who is his power of attorney and caregiver. She says that her father is increasingly confused and verbally aggressive. He is admitted to the hospital and found to have a urinary tract infection. Intravenous antibiotics are initiated and he appears less disoriented and agitated but remains unable to recall nursing instructions throughout the day and is fairly nonverbal. Three days into his treatment, the antibiotic order expires and is missed by the physician, nurses, and pharmacy staff. The patient receives no medication for 2 days before the error is noted and corrected. There are no consequences to this error other than extension of his hospital stay by 2 days. Which of the following is the most appropriate course of action?

clues.note: Advanced dementia means the patient lacks decision-making capacity. Disclosure and decisions must therefore go through his surrogate, not the patient himself. This reframes the whole ethics question.
The daughter is the durable power of attorney = the legal surrogate decision-maker. She is the correct person to receive disclosure of the error on the patient's behalf.

clues.text: remains unable to recall nursing instructions throughout the day and is fairly nonverbal
clues.note: Confirms the patient cannot understand or retain information — he lacks capacity to receive a meaningful disclosure. This is why you disclose to the surrogate.

clues.text: There are no consequences to this error other than extension of his hospital stay
clues.note: The 'no real harm' framing is the trap that tempts non-disclosure. Ethics requires HONEST disclosure of errors regardless of harm — even a 2-day extension is a consequence (cost, infection risk). Lack of harm never justifies hiding an error.

whatMatters: The error must be disclosed honestly with an apology — that part is non-negotiable. Because the patient has advanced dementia and lacks capacity, disclosure goes to his legal surrogate (his daughter, the power of attorney) — answer C.
strategy: Error-disclosure questions: always disclose, in person, promptly, with an apology, regardless of harm. Then ask WHO receives it — if the patient lacks capacity, disclose to the surrogate (POA/guardian). Eliminate any option that hides the error or substitutes a refund for honesty.

options.text: Do not disclose the error as the patient was not harmed
Do not disclose the error but arrange for the hospital to not charge for the extra days
Inform the daughter and disclose the error with an apology
Inform the patient and disclose the error with an apology
Say nothing until the incident is discussed with nursing and pharmacy staff

eliminations.label: non-disclosure 'because no harm' — violates honesty
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: withholding because the patient wasn't physically harmed. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: full disclosure is required regardless of harm; the extra stay still carries cost and hospital-acquired-infection risk. ✅ Correct if: ethics ever permitted hiding errors — it doesn't.

eliminations.label: hide it but waive charges — a bribe, not disclosure
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: quietly fixing the bill instead of telling the family. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: financial adjustment does not substitute for honest disclosure and apology; concealment breaches trust. ✅ Correct if: the question asked about appropriate follow-up AFTER disclosure (waiving charges can be reasonable then).

eliminations.label: inform the patient — he lacks capacity
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: disclosing directly to the patient. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: advanced dementia removes capacity to understand; he is nonverbal and cannot retain information, so the surrogate must be informed. ✅ Correct if: the patient had decision-making capacity.

eliminations.label: delay/say nothing pending staff discussion — needless concealment
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: waiting to investigate with nursing/pharmacy before telling anyone. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: the error is already known and confirmed; delaying disclosure isn't justified. Investigating the system is separate from promptly disclosing to the family. ✅ Correct if: there were genuine doubt that an error even occurred.

reasoning: A real medication error occurred (2 missed days)
Disclosure with apology is required regardless of harm
Patient has advanced dementia → lacks capacity to receive it
Disclose to the legal surrogate (daughter/POA) → answer C
mechanism: Ethical and professional standards require timely, in-person disclosure of medical errors with an apology, regardless of whether harm occurred — it preserves trust and is associated with neutral-to-positive effects on the physician-patient relationship. When the patient lacks decision-making capacity (advanced dementia), the durable power of attorney/health-care proxy is the appropriate recipient of disclosure and decisions. Concealment, or substituting a financial gesture for honesty, is unethical.

difficulty.label: 📚 Pure knowledge gap
difficulty.message: First Aid Ethics > Disclosure of medical errors and Decision-making capacity/surrogates: disclose promptly with apology regardless of harm; surrogate hierarchy (POA/health-care proxy → spouse → adult children, etc.) when capacity is lost.

difficulty.label: 🧭 Understanding the question
difficulty.message: Split the question in two: (1) disclose or not? → always disclose, (2) to whom? → surrogate if no capacity. Answer C satisfies both.

difficulty.label: 🔤 Vocabulary
difficulty.message: Unsure on decision-making capacity, durable power of attorney, surrogate, health-care proxy, disclosure? Tell me which and I'll explain.

difficulty.label: 🧲 Pulled by a distractor
difficulty.message: Option D ('inform the patient') is the near-miss — it gets disclosure right but the recipient wrong. The dementia/capacity clues redirect to the daughter.

difficulty.label: ✍️ Something else
difficulty.message: Correct (C). Bank the two-part rule: always disclose with apology; route to the surrogate when the patient lacks capacity.

keywords.back: Disclose with apology to the surrogate (POA)

keywords.front: Disclose errors even if no harm?
keywords.back: Yes — always, promptly, in person, with apology

keywords.front: Who decides when capacity is lost
keywords.back: Durable power of attorney / health-care proxy
Proxy/POA → spouse → adult children → parents → siblings
Unethical — not a substitute for honesty
Understand, appreciate, reason, communicate a choice

firstAid.detail: Ethics: full, timely, in-person disclosure with apology regardless of harm; root-cause analysis is separate from disclosure.
firstAid.location: Ethics: full, timely, in-person disclosure with apology regardless of harm; root-cause analysis is separate from disclosure.

firstAid.detail: Ethics: capacity assessment, durable power of attorney/health-care proxy, surrogate hierarchy when capacity is lost.
firstAid.location: Ethics: capacity assessment, durable power of attorney/health-care proxy, surrogate hierarchy when capacity is lost.

firstAid.detail: Ethics: why non-disclosure and financial substitution breach professional duties; truth-telling and trust.
firstAid.location: Ethics: why non-disclosure and financial substitution breach professional duties; truth-telling and trust.

memoryHook: Always tell + say sorry — to the right person. No capacity → tell the POA (the daughter).
